coredata.dc:description: Photo redox atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) represents an excellent technique for forming multifunctional polymers. However, there was little report on photo redox ATRP of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Herein, we reported that introduced GQDs to ATRP in DMF solvent for the first time, significantly reducing the amount of copper catalyst (<500 ppm) required for the synthesis of alkali lignin-poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)-poly(N, N-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)-poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) (AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2). The copolymer had molecular weight of 46,884 and polydispersity index (PDI) of 1.07. The micelles self-assembled from AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 in aqueous solution served as an excellent template for the simultaneous in situ preparation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and the encapsulation of the hydrophobic anticancer drug camptothecin (CPT). By adjusting the mole ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3], a series of AgNPs with controllable sizes (2.5–20.6 nm) could be obtained. AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT exhibited temperature and pH-sensitive release properties, releasing 80 % of the drug in an acidic and 37 °C tumor environment within 120 h. Furthermore, 150 μg/mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT effectively killed 75 % of cells within 48 h. 800 μg/mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT demonstrated highly efficient antibacterial effects. All these results suggest its potential application value in synergistic antitumor and antibacterial treatment system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Mechanism of photo redox ATRP using GQDs as a photocatalyst and its deoxygenation effect. (B) TEM image of GQDs (scale bar = 50 nm) (inset left, the aqueous dispersion of GQDs; inset middle, the digital photo of GQDs; inset right, the emission behavior of the GQDs aqueous dispersion under 365 nm UV light). (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (in blue) and the photoluminescence spectrum of GQDs under 460 nm excitation (in red). (D) Relationship between the fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs under 460 nm excitation and different concentrations of the CuBr2/Me6TREN complex. (E) Plots of the I0/I ratio versus the concentration of the quencher; I0 and I correspond to the emission intensity in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively.
1H NMR spectra of (A) AL-Br and (B) AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2. (C) FT-IR spectra of AL, AL-Br, and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2. (D) GPC curve of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2.

(A) Graphs of intensity ratios (I 334/I 332) as function of logarithm of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 concentrations in aqueous solution. (B) The temperature-dependent transmittance graph of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2. The effect of pH value on the D h (C) and zeta potential (D) of the micellar solution of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) zeta potential diagrams at different mole ratios of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3]. (C) XRD of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag ([DMAEMA]:[AgNO3] = 6). TEM images of AgNPs stabilized by AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 at mole ratios of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3] being (D) 9, (E) 6, and (F) 3.
(A) LC and EE at different mass ratios. (B) TEM of CPT-loaded micelles. (C) In vitro release curves under different pH and temperature environments, and (D) release mechanism.

In vitro cytotoxicity of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag against MCF-7 cells (A) and HepG2 cells (C) for 48 h. In vitro cytotoxicity of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT against MCF-7 cells (B) and HepG2 cells (D). Fluorescence microscopic images of control group (E) and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT against HepG2 cells at 48 h (F). Scale bars are all 100 μm. Significant differences were analyzed using Student's t-test (n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001).
Antibacterial activity of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag, and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT against E. coli (A) and S. aureus (B) after 18 h.
Schematic illustration of anti-tumor and anti-bacterial therapy of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-induced photo redox ATRP synthesis of lignin-based copolymers for the fabrication of silver-bearing and camptothecin-loaded micelles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo redox atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) represents an excellent technique for forming multifunctional polymers. However, there was little report on photo redox ATRP of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Herein, we reported that introduced GQDs to ATRP in DMF solvent for the first time, significantly reducing the amount of copper catalyst (<500 ppm) required for the synthesis of alkali lignin-poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)-poly(N, N-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)-poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) (AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2). The copolymer had molecular weight of 46,884 and polydispersity index (PDI) of 1.07. The micelles self-assembled from AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 in aqueous solution served as an excellent template for the simultaneous in situ preparation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and the encapsulation of the hydrophobic anticancer drug camptothecin (CPT). By adjusting the mole ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3], a series of AgNPs with controllable sizes (2.5–20.6 nm) could be obtained. AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT exhibited temperature and pH-sensitive release properties, releasing 80 % of the drug in an acidic and 37 °C tumor environment within 120 h. Furthermore, 150 μg/mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT effectively killed 75 % of cells within 48 h. 800 μg/mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT demonstrated highly efficient antibacterial effects. All these results suggest its potential application value in synergistic antitumor and antibacterial treatment system.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, amphiphilic polymer nano-micelle drug delivery systems have become one of the key directions in developing new drug delivery systems. These systems could flexibly carry drugs and assist in overcoming physiological and pathological barriers to achieve sustained and targeted delivery, thereby enhancing the efficacy and reducing the toxicity of antitumor treatments [1–4]. Stimuli-sensitive anticancer drug delivery system was a type of intelligent drug delivery system that could undergo corresponding physical or chemical changes under the influence of endogenous stimuli (such as pH, redox, enzymes, etc.) or exogenous stimuli (such as temperature, magnetic fields, electric fields, light, etc.), thereby releasing the drug, which has attracted widespread attention recently [5–8]. Specific chemical structures were introduced into the polymers to achieve drug release in response to stimuli, enabling rapid drug release through changes in these chemical structures under stimulation. For instance, Xu et al. synthesized an amphiphilic three-arm star-shaped polymer C3-(PS-b-PNIPAM) to encapsulate the anticancer drug 10-hydroxycamptothecin. The micelles were temperature-sensitive and could efficiently release the drug at a temperature above the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) [9]. However, as more stimuli-sensitive elements were introduced, the complexity of polymer synthesis increased. Thus most of the current research still focuses on dual-stimuli-sensitive drug delivery [10,11]. For example, Xiong et al. reported a polymer crosslinked micelles with pH-sensitive groups and disulfide bonds in the main chain. Under normal tissue conditions, the cumulative drug release rate was only 23 % after 72 h. However, it could release 90 % in a tumor environment with weak acidity and 10 mM GSH after 72 h [12].

Many bacteria were present in human tumors [13,14]. Certain bacteria presented in tumor tissue could reduce the effectiveness of chemotherapy drugs and antibiotics, potentially increasing the risk of tumor spread and recurrence. Modern medical treatment strategies often combined anti-tumor drugs with anti-infective drugs to treat both tumor diseases and bacterial infections. However, the concurrent use of multiple drugs would increase the risk of toxic side effects and the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria, posing challenges to treatment. Recently, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been widely used in biomedicine, antimicrobial materials, etc. [15,16]. Currently, a widely used technique for in-situ preparation of AgNPs by amphiphilic polymer micelles is chemical reduction method, in which Ag+ are reduced to AgNPs by adding reducing agents such as sodium borohydride, sodium sodium citrate, hydrazine hydrate and tetrabutylammonium borohydride (TBAB) to a solution of silver salts [17,18]. Huang et al. designed and synthesized the amphiphilic diblock copolymer PCL-b-PAsp and prepared polymeric micelle-loaded AgNPs with a particle size of about 90 nm in the presence of sodium borohydride an external reducing agent [19]. Adding external reducing agents and utilizing the stabilizing effect of micelles could obtain a specific size of AgNPs. However, using external reducing agents increased the post-treatment process and may also lead to toxic side effects in the anticancer and antibacterial systems. The literature reported that polymers containing amine groups could serve as both reducing agents and stabilizers, enabling a one-step in situ preparation of metal nanoparticles, including AgNPs and AuNPs [20–22]. For example, Lin et al. synthesized linear and 4-armed star polymers consisting of three blocks of N,N-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA), hydroxyethyl methacrylate-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) and poly(ethylene glycol) monomethyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA), which not only could directly reduce Ag+ in situ in the aqueous phase without requiring an additional reductant, but also yield AgNPs with sizes ranging from 2.5 to 20.6 nm by varying the amount of silver nitrate. [23].

In recent years, photo redox atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) has attracted increasing attention from researchers because of its mild reaction conditions, broad range of monomers, and the ability to control the polymerization process with “on/off” light and ensure polymer purity, etc. [24–26]. However, the majority of photocatalysts currently employed in photo redox ATRP are semiconductor nanoparticles, organic dyes, and organic catalysts, which have drawbacks including toxicity, high cost, and complexity in preparation. These limitations could restrict their application in the field of biology [27–31]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), due to their photo redox electron transfer capabilities and advantages like low toxicity and good biocompatibility, were utilized as photocatalysts for ATRP [32]. Recently, Pang et al. first attempted to introduce CQDs into an aqueous photo redox ATRP system. The photoexcited facilitated electron transfer between CQDs and [Cu(II)X/L], leading to the formation of [Cu(I)X/L]. [Cu(I)X/L] initiated the photo redox ATRP process with alkyl bromides, synthesizing poly(hydroxyethyl acrylate) (PHEA) with a controllable process and narrow PDI eventually [33]. Compared to CQDs, selecting GQDs with unique two-dimensional structures, smaller sizes, and superior electron transfer properties, was of great significance for broadening the selection of photocatalysts for ATRP. However, there have been no reports on using GQDs as catalysts for ATRP.

Herein, we utilized GQDs as photocatalysts to significantly reduce the amount of copper catalyst needed. With abundantly available alkali lignin (AL) as the core of the polymer, we sequentially coupled temperature-sensitive monomers (NIPAM), pH-sensitive monomers (DMAEMA), and hydrophilic monomers (PEGMA) through photo redox ATRP. It was also the first report utilizing GQDs as photocatalysts and AL as copolymer core for consecutive photo redox ATRP. The copolymer, alkali lignin-poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)-poly(N,N-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)-poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate) (AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2), formed micelles through self-assembly in aqueous solution, which could simultaneously prepare AgNPs in situ and encapsulate the hydrophobic anticancer drug molecule camptothecin (CPT), achieving targeted anticancer and antibacterial therapy at the cancer site (Scheme 1 ).

In AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2, the AL and the PNIPAM core along with parts of the PDMAEMA middle layer provided spaces for the encapsulation of drug molecules to increase drug loading capacity. The hydrophilic PEGMA block formed the shell of the micelles, serving a protective role. The PNIPAM segment, constituting the core of the micelles, could respond to the temperature at the tumor site. The PDMAEMA segment in the middle layer acted as both a reducing agent and a stabilizer, which could reduce Ag+ in situ to prepare stable AgNPs, overcoming the drawbacks of needing organic solvents and other reducing agents. It also acted as a pH-sensitive segment that protonated and released the drug under acidic conditions in tumors, achieving effective cancer treatment. Our study focused on the synthesis of polymers through photo redox ATRP, the preparation of micelles and AgNPs, along with the drug release performance, anticancer activity, and antibacterial properties of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT. The results indicated that the multifunctional drug delivery system holds significant potential for combined anticancer and antibacterial therapy.

N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), tris(2-dimethylaminoethyl) (Me6TREN), copper(II) bromide (CuBr2), sodium bromide (NaBr), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2EDTA), alkali lignin (AL) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Triethylamine (TEA), 2-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (BIBB), sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Anhui Zesheng Technology Co.,Ltd. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were purchased from Shanghai Shangmu Technology Co., Ltd. LB liquid medium was supplied by Guangzhou Rongman Biotechnology Co., Ltd. CCK8 assay kit, 0.25 % trypsin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS) containing 10 % were obtained from the Hangzhou Yanqu Information Technology Co., Ltd. Penicillin-streptomycin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co.Ltd. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Gibco) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. Calcein-AM/PI Double Stain Kit was purchased from Beibo Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The MCF-7 cells (human breast cancer cells) used in this experiment were purchased from Bena Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Henan, China). The HepG2 cells (human liver cancer cells) used in this experiment were purchased from Procell Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA, M n = 475, n = 8) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N,N-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. They were purified by passing through a neutral alumina column to remove inhibitors and were used as received. All other chemical reagents were commercial products and used as received.

A 500 mL dried beaker was charged with a stirrer, unpurified AL (8.00 g, 40 mmol) and NaOH (200 mL, 2 M), stirring for 10 min. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 2 M) was then added dropwise to the solution until the pH was adjusted to 6.0, the solution was centrifuged and the supernatant was collected. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 2 M) was added again until the pH was adjusted to 4.0. After centrifugation, the supernatant was taken, washed with water, and filtered by suction repeatedly three times. Finally, it was lyophilized for 48 h to obtain a dark brown powdery solid (purified AL).

A 250 mL dried three-neck flask was charged with a stirrer and purified AL (1.56 g, 0.70 mmol) sealed with a rubber stopper, evacuated as well as purged with nitrogen three times. DMF (106.50 mL) and TEA (3.94 mL) were sequentially injected into the flask. Subsequently, BIBB (3.52 mL) was added slowly under an ice-water bath to react at 0 °C for 2 h, and then for 22 h at room temperature. After the reaction was completed, the resulting solution was poured into 1 L of deionized water, washed repeatedly, centrifuged three times, and lyophilized for 48 h to obtain a brown powdery solid finally.

A 10 mL dried single-neck flask with a small headspace was charged with a stirrer, the initiator AL-Br (0.11 g, 47.50 μmol), the first monomer NIPAM, (0.32 g, 2.85 mmol), and DMF (3 mL), was added and stirred thoroughly to ensure dissolved entirely. Next, an aqueous solution of CuBr2 (1.40 μmol, 0.02 M, 67 μL), Me6TREN (223 μmol, 60 μL), Na2EDTA (3.00 mg, 6.34 μmol), NaBr (1.00 mg, 6.34 μmol), and GQDs (1 mg/mL, 250 μL) were sequentially added, and stirred for 5 min to form the catalyst complex. The photocatalytic reaction of GQDs was carried out under blue light at 460 nm. Hence, the reaction system was transferred into a preheated 60 °C oil bath for stirring and reaction in advance. After 9 h, when the system's viscosity increased, the temperature was reduced to 50 °C, and the reaction could be continued by adding the second monomer DMAEMA (4.75 mmol). After 15 h, when the viscosity was greater, the temperature was reduced to 30 °C once again, and the hydrophilic monomer PEGMA (4.75 mmol) was added for 12 h. When reaction completed, the flask was cooled, 50 mL DMF was added to dissolve it, and the catalyst was removed by filtration through a neutral alumina column (using DMF as the eluent). After rotary evaporation and concentration, the crude product solution was slowly added to an amount of cold n-hexane ten times its volume to precipitate, followed by dissolution-precipitation, repeated three times, eventually obtained a yellow viscous solid by drying under vacuum at 80 °C for one day.

Pyrene acted as a probe to detect the critical micelle concentration (CMC) of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 by fluorescence probe technology. 0.1 mL pyrene/acetone solution (1.2 × 10−5 M) in multiple volumetric flasks was prepared and then evaporated the acetone, then added aqueous solution of micelles (0.0001–0.01 mg/mL) to obtain the final concentration of pyrene in the solution was 6 × 10−7 M.

Micelles were prepared by dialysis. 100 mg AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 was dissolved in 25 mL DMF. AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 micelles were obtained after 48 h dialysis and 48 h freeze-dried. Micelles samples at a concentration of 2 mg/mL were prepared in ultrapure water solution. The relationship curve between transmittance and temperature at a wavelength of 500 nm was determined by a variable temperature UV–visible spectrophotometer. 1 mg/mL polymer solution was prepared with deionized water and the pH value of the micellar solution was adjusted by adding NaOH (0.01 M) or HCl (0.01 M) to explore the pH-sensitive self-assembly behavior.

Taking the mole ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3] = 6 as an example, 7 mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 micelle solution (2 mg/mL, [DMAEMA] = 2.2 × 10−1 mmol) and 3 mL deionized water were mixed evenly in a 50 mL flask. Then, 1 mL AgNO3 solution ([AgNO3] = 0.37 × 10−1 mmol) was slowly added dropwise to the flask, thus obtaining a brown-yellow reaction solution by 48 h stirring under the condition of darkness at 25 °C. After 48 h of dialysis and 48 h of freeze-drying, the final product obtained was AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag micelles. The preparation methods of other ratios of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag micelles were the same. The crystalline form, content, and morphology of AgNPs were determined by XRD, TGA, and TEM.

AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles were prepared by dialysis. Firstly, CPT (0.30–1.00 mg) and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag (2.00 mg) were dissolved in 0.4 mL DMF and stirred for 2 h to obtain the CPT solution. Then, the CPT solution and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag were mixed and continued stirring for 6 h. After 48 h of dialysis and 48 h of lyophilization, the AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles were obtained. The partical size and morphology of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag, and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT were characterized by DLS, TEM, UV–vis.

The UV–vis absorbance of CPT/PBS solution was measured at 371 nm with PBS as a blank control to obtain the standard curve of CPT. The absorbance of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT solutions at 371 nm was determined by UV–vis. The CPT loading content and encapsulation efficiency were calculated by substituting the standard curve of CPT in PBS. Formulas for calculating LC (loading capacity) and EE (encapsulation efficiency) were as follows:
(1)

wt

Weight of loaded drug Weight of drug loaded micelle
× 100 %
(2)

wt
=
Weight of loaded drug
Weight of drug in feed
× 100 %

3 mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles (0.5 mg/mL) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4) or acetic acid buffer (pH = 5.0) were transferred into a dialysis bag (M w = 3500), subsequently placed in 100 mL PBS or acetic acid buffer solution. Keeping moderate stirring at 30 °C/37 °C and 100 rpm. 3 mL samples were taken at regular intervals, and then absorbance at 371 nm was determined after filtration using 0.45 μm filters. Also, 3 mL of fresh buffer was replenished. The cumulative release of CPT was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
E r
=
V e
∑ 1
n − 1
C i
+
V 0

× 100 %
E r

C i

m CPT
: Weight of CPT in micelles, mg; n : The number of times the buffer is replaced.

The test aimed to explore the anti-tumor activity of blank micelles, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag micelles, and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles against MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells by CCK8 assay. Cell culture medium was DMEM, 10 % FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin. A incubator (Beijing SangYi Laboratory Instrument Research Institute, WIGGENSWCI-180) with 5 % CO2 was used to culture cells at 37 °C. Before using cells, they were harvested by trypsin and re-suspended in fresh medium. MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells were then seeded on a 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells/well/100 μL) and incubated for 24 h in a constant temperature incubator (Beijing SangYi Laboratory Instrument Research Institute, WIGGENSWCI-180) at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. Then, different range of concentrations of blank micelles (0, 5, 10, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL), AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag (0, 5, 10, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL), AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80 and 150 μg/mL) were added to the above 96-well plate and continued to culture for 48 h. Next, the old culture medium from the wells was carefully washed three times with cold PBS, and 100 μL fresh culture medium was added to each well. Subsequently, 10 μL CCK8 solution was added to each well and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Finally, the optical density (OD) at 450 nm was measured by using an ELISA reader. The formula for calculating the cell survival rate was as follows:
(4)
Cell viability =
OD sample
−
OD Blank
OD Control
−
OD Blank
× 100 %

HepG2 cells were planted into confocal dishes with a density of 2 × 105 cells/well and co-cultured with control sample or 150 μg/mL AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT in same constant temperature incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 overnight. Cell apoptosis was detected using the Calcein-AM/PI Double Stain Kit (live/dead stain kit). Cells were washed three times with buffer solution and stained with staining solution for 15 min in the dark. Afterward, cells were washed three times with buffer solution. Fluorescence images were acquired by ZEISS Axio Vert.A1 software (ZEISS, Germany).

S. aureus (Gram-positive) and E. coli (Gram-negative) were selected to detect antibacterial activity. S. aureus and E. coli were both purchased from Guangzhou TangSui Technology Co., Ltd. Taking 20 mL LB medium and sterilizing under high pressure at 121 °C. Thawing frozen strains, using an inoculation ring to take about 10 μL bacterial solution, then added it to the sterilized tapered flask with 10 mL LB medium. After sealing the flask, the sample was placed into a shaker at 37 °C with a stirring speed of 80 rpm and shaken overnight. Subsequently, the suspension of bacteria cultured overnight in fresh liquid culture was diluted 10-fold and then its OD 600 was measured. After dilution, the bacterial density was 1 × 106 CFU/mL. Precisely added 200 μL of the targeted bacterial suspension to each well of a 48-well culture plate, followed by 200 μL blank micelles, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag micelles, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles were respectively added to each well (4 parallels for each group). The cells were cultured for 18 h at 37 °C with a stirring speed of 80 rpm in a shaker, then the OD 600 for each group was determined by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader.

The mass of the sample was measured using a Shimadzu AUW120D electronic analytical balance. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker AVANCE III 400 MHz superconducting Fourier spectrometer. The GPC measurements were carried out in THF using a WATERS 1515 instrument. Polystyrene (PS) standards with narrow and well-defined molecular weight distributions were used as references and liquid chromatography (THF) was delivered at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min with the column oven temperature maintained at 35 °C. The structure of the polymer was examined using a Thermo Scientific™ Nicolet™ iS50R research Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer with a potassium bromide (KBr) sample holder. The particle size in the aqueous solution was measured using the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS dynamic light scattering instrument. Each sample was filtered through a 0.45 μm filter before testing. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), operating at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA, with a scanning range of 5° to 80° and a step size of 0.02°. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a STA449F5 analyzer, heating from 25 °C to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min. The morphology of the micelles was characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Thermo). The standard curve of CPT and the release amount of CPT were measured using a TU1901 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.). The optical density (OD) values of the sample solutions were determined using an ELISA reader(EPOCH2, Biotek). Cytotoxicity tests were conducted using ELISA reader (TECAN, model: SPARK 10 M).

All the quantitative results were carried out at least three samples. The data were analyzed using Origin, followed by a Student's t-test, one-way analysis of variance and two-way analysis of variance. The statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the photo redox ATRP of carbon quantum dots (CDs), as shown in Fig. 1A, GQDs generated hole-electron pairs, simultaneously transitioning from the ground state to the excited state under blue light (460 nm) irradiation. The excited quantum dots GQDs* acted as a reducing agent in the reaction system, reducing the deactivated species [Cu(II)Br2/Me6TREN] to the activated species [Cu(I)Br/Me6TREN]. Meanwhile, the initiator AL-Br was halogenated by the low-valent active species [Cu(I)Br/Me6TREN], generating active free radicals AL• and the high-valent deactivated agent [Cu(II)Br2/Me6TREN]. AL• initiated monomer polymerization to form the chain growth free radical AL-Mn• during the chain growth phase. In succession, the chain growth free radical AL-Mn• seized the halogen atom Br from [Cu(II)Br2/Me6TREN] during the chain termination phase, forming a temporarily deactivated macromolecular halide AL-Mn•-Br and the activated species [Cu(I)Br/Me6TREN]. Consequently, AL-Mn•-Br and [Cu(I)Br/Me6TREN] regularly underwent chain growth, initiation, and termination reactions until target macromolecular products were obtained. Moreover, adding a hole scavenger (Na2EDTA) could increase the lifespan of GQDs and promote electron transfer, enhancing the polymerization rate. Herein, GQDs were selected as photocatalysts (the yellow powder in Fig. 1B, inset middle). GQDs of yellow powdery could be well dispersed in an aqueous medium, and the solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV light at λ = 365 nm (Fig. 1B, inset right). The TEM image (Fig. 1B) showed that the average diameter of the GQDs was 4.86 ± 0.5 nm. GQDs exhibited significant absorption in the ultraviolet region due to π − π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) electronic transitions. In addition, the absorption spectrum of GQDs in the visible light region showed a broader characteristic curve. (Fig. 1C) A fluorescence quenching experiment was carried out to further explore the ATRP photocatalytic activity of GQDs in aqueous media. The [Cu(II)Br2/Me6TREN] complex was added to an aqueous solution of GQDs to measure the fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs excited at 460 nm. As expected, the PL intensity decreased with an increase in the [Cu(II)Br2/Me6TREN] complex, indicating electron transfer between the GQDs and the complex.

Thus, an amphiphilic copolymer AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 was synthesized by GQDs catalysted photo redox ATRP in this study. (Scheme 2 ). Initially, AL was used as the macromolecular core and then was brominated to act as the AL-Br initiator of the polymerization reaction. Under blue light at 460 nm, using DMF solvent, a dual catalytic system consisting of CuBr2/Me6TREN and GQDs, with Na2EDTA serving as a hole scavenger and NaBr as a halogenating agent, sequentially initiated the temperature-sensitive monomer NIPAM, the pH-sensitive monomer DMAEMA, and the hydrophilic monomer PEGMA to obtain AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 by photo redox ATRP.

1H NMR was used to characterize the structure and composition of AL (Fig. S1), AL-Br (Fig. 2A), and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 (Fig. 2B). The hydrogen proton signals of AL aromatic rings were primarily located between 6.5 and 7.3 ppm. Peaks of AL-Br at 2.1 ppm (a) and 1.9 ppm (b) were respectively attributed to the hydrogen proton peaks on -(CH3)2-Br of “a” site and “b” site, indicating AL was brominated successfully. 1H NMR of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 was shown in Fig. 2B. Specifically, 0.9 ppm (d) and 4.07 ppm (c) were characteristic proton peaks on HN-CH (CH3)2 and -NH-CH- of PNIPAM respectively; 2.3 ppm (i) and 2.6 ppm (h) were the characteristic proton peaks of -N(CH3)2 and -CH2-N-(CH3)2 of PDMAEMA respectively; while 3.6 ppm (l, m) were the characteristic proton peaks on the -CH2-CH2- next to ester group of PPEGMA. Additionally, the disappearance of the CC peak (5.8–6.5 ppm) indicated that monomers have been thoroughly removed. Based on the above analysis, it could be inferred that the copolymer was successfully synthesized. The degree of polymerization of PNIPAM (x), PDMAEMA (y), and PPEGMA (z) on each arm of the polymer could be obtained by analyzing the integral area ratio of the characteristic peaks (I c/I a ′, I i/I a ′和I l/I a ′), which were 4, 37 and 37. Then the molecular weight (M n/NMR = 50,117) of the polymer products could be calculated.

The FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2C) could further demonstrate the successful synthesis of the AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2, in which the characteristic peak of CBr in AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 structure was located at 650 cm−1, peaks at 1640 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 corresponded to CO and NH stretching vibrations of PNIPAM respectively, while the peak at 1360 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of the isopropyl group (HN-C(CH3)2) of PNIPAM. Meanwhile, the appearance of peaks at 1250 cm−1 and 2760 cm−1 respectively corresponded to the expansion vibration of CO and CH of PDMAEMA, while peaks at 2860 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of CH and CO of PPEGMA. The GPC curve of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 exhibited a symmetrical single-peak shape with a molecular weight of 46,884 and polydispersity index (PDI) as low as 1.07, indicating uniform and narrow molecular weight distribution, along with good controllability during photo redox ATRP (Fig. 2D). The result of GPC (M n/GPC = 46,884) was close to the calculations obtained from 1H NMR (M n/NMR = 50,117), suggesting AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 with different compositions was synthesized successfully.

The CMC value was an important parameter to measure the stability of amphiphilic copolymers. Generally speaking, micelles with low CMC exhibted good stability in blood circulation, maintaining the integrity of the drug-carrying system. The CMC of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 was tested by the fluorescent probe technique. As the environmental polarity decreased, the pyrene excitation spectrum (3rd peak) gradually shifted from 332 nm to 334 nm. Meanwhile, the increase of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 concentration also increased the excitation spectrum intensity of pyrene. Fig. 3A illustrated that the intensity ratio of I
334/I 332 varied with concentration, and the turning point in the sharp increase in I 334/I 332 was the CMC value. It could be calculated that the CMC value for AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 was 0.0069 mg/mL. The low CMC value of the micelles demonstrated its good stability in water. The TEM image of blank AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 micelles with a size of 40–110 nm was shown in Fig. S2, exhibiting a relatively uniform morphology.

NIPAM served as the temperature-sensitive component of the micelles' structure, capable of altering the hydrophobic-hydrophilic balance of the chemical composition. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the transmittance of the micelles solution diminished as the temperature rose from 24 °C to 36 °C. That was due to the PNIPAM molecular chains shrinking when the micelles exceeded the low critical solution temperature (LCST = 33.1 °C), causing an enhanced hydrophobicity of the micelles and thereby resulting in a decrease in transmittance [34]. The slight variation in transmittance may be attributed to the low proportion of the thermosensitive monomer NIPAM, resulting in a weaker temperature response.

The effect of pH change on the D h and zeta potential of the micelles solution was shown in Fig. 3C & D. The PDMAEMA blocks were completely deprotonated and shrunk when the pH > 7.4, forming the inner core of the micelles in conjunction with PNIPAM to yield structurally compact micelles. Thus, the change of pH had little effect on the D h of the micelles solution. However, the potential exhibited a negative value due to the increasing number of OH− in the system at that time. D h and zeta potential of the micelles solution increased significantly as the pH gradually decreased from 7.4 to 4.0. It could be explained by the constant protonation of the side chain tertiary amine groups of PDMAEMA, which enhanced the hydrophilicity of PDMAEMA and extended into the solution phase, leading to the swelling of the micelle. As pH < 4.0, the complete protonation of the PDMAEMA block and the electrostatic repulsion between the chains reduced the number of micelles aggregation, resulting in a slight dissociation of the micellar structure [35], hence the D h and zeta potential decreased.

In summary, the size and potential changes of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 self-assembled micelles demonstrated that it possessed well pH-sensitive properties. A suitable pH-sensitive range, ideal particle size, and appropriate zeta potential could enhance the interaction between micelles and cell membranes, thereby facilitating drug utilization efficiency and therapeutic efficacy.

Firstly, AgNO3 solution was added to AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 micellar solution (2 mg/mL). Subsequently, the lone-pair electron on the nitrogen (N) atom in the tertiary amine group of the molecular chain could coordinate with Ag+, thereby forming the (Ag+)-PDMAEMA complex [36]. Next, Ag+ reduced in situ to be Ag atom during the nucleation stage and finally generated AgNPs. However, hydrophilic PPEGMA served as the shell of the micelles; it not only effectively enhanced the shell density but also further improved the stability of AgNPs. The stability of the micelles within the system depended on the stabilization of the micelles on AgNPs and the thermodynamic equilibrium state of AgNPs aggregation [37,38]. With fewer AgNPs, the spatial stabilization of the polymer could be improved and aggregation of AgNPs could be prevented. However, with an increased number of AgNPs, the polymer's stabilizing ability towards the nanoparticles diminished. Meanwhile, the increase in collision opportunities between AgNPs also led to the rise of AgNPs' size.

The effect on the size and potential of AgNPs was investigated by changing the mole ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3]. The UV–vis (Fig. 4A) showed that with the increasing ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3], peaks of UV absorption at 420 nm, 418 nm, and 414 nm, respectively, corresponding to an average particle size of 20.6 nm, 11.2 nm and 2.5 nm of the TEM image (Fig. 4D & E & F). It indicated a gradual decrease in particle size due to the varying quantities of AgNPs formed through amine coordination reduction. As shown in Fig. 4B, the content of AgNO3 decreased with the increase of the mole ratio of [DMAEMA]:[AgNO3], the amount of AgNO3 available for the coordination reduction reaction with PDMAEMA also decreased, suggesting more PDMAEMA with positive electricity were not involved in the reaction. Thus, the zeta potential increased. The results of the UV–vis and zeta potential analyses were consistent. The XRD pattern (Fig. 4C) showed peaks at 38.1°, 44.5°, 64.7°, and 77.6°, respectively corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal faces certified the face-centered cubic structure of AgNPs.

When discussing the antibacterial properties of AgNPs, size and concentration are indeed two critical factors. Attaining a homogeneous distribution of AgNPs at elevated concentrations is pivotal for realizing excellent antimicrobial effects. Within a specific concentration range, the higher the concentration of AgNPs, the greater the specific surface area, results in a more efficient anti-bacterial effect. However, too high concentrations of AgNPs caused to an aggregation between AgNPs and reduced the surface area in contact with microorganisms, decreasing their antibacterial property and increasing cytotoxicity, thereby resulting in a greater threat to biological cells during sterilization. Therefore, the ratio of [PDMAEMA]:[AgNO3] = 6 was selected for later experiments. Compared blank polymer and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag by TGA (Fig. S3), AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag contained 10 % Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CPT-loaded micelles
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of drug to polymer was the major factor affecting the LC and EE of the micelles. For CPT-loaded micelles with different mass ratios of CPT to AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag, the CPT/PBS standard curve (Fig. S4) was prepared, and then the LC and EE of micelles were tested by UV–vis. As shown in Fig. 5A, the LC of micelles with four different mass ratios were 4.3 %, 8.7 %, 13.7 %, and 16.1 %, respectively. LC gradually increased with the increased amount of CPT, but EE began to decline when mass ratios exceeded 35 %. The phenomenon was attributed to the saturation value of the CPT in the micelles, the EE would decrease when exceeded the saturation value. Taking LC and EE into consideration, the mass ratio 35/100 was selected for the later experiment. The TEM image of the CPT-loaded micelles was shown in Fig. 5B. Compared with the blank micelles (Fig. S2), the size of the CPT-loaded micelles increased to 80–150 nm with monodisperse spherical morphology. The former research had mainly focused on developing intelligent drug delivery systems with pH-responsive designs. In our study, we introduced the temperature-sensitive monomers NIPAM and pH-sensitive DMAEMA into the system, enabling the targeted release of CPT in the tumor microenvironment. As shown in Fig. 5C, the cumulative release of CPT was no more than 25 % under simulated normal tissue (37 °C, pH = 7.4) in 24 h without sudden drug release. The tertiary amine group of the deprotonated PDMAEMA at pH = 7.4 improved the hydrophobic properties of the micelles, thus increasing the resistance to outward drug diffusion. When the pH value was reduced to lysosomal microenvironment (37 °C, pH = 5.0), PDMAEMA were fully protonated became hydrophilic blocks and affected by electrostatic repulsion, resulting in micelles swelling and then released CPT rapidly, with cumulative releases of CPT were 46 % and 83 % in 24 h and 108 h, respectively. The drug release rate at 30 °C was slightly lower than 37 °C, indicating that the release rate was enhanced near the PNIPAM transition temperature. Therefore, CPT-loaded micelles could maintain reasonable drug encapsulation and release, and could also regulate the release behavior by altering environmental temperature or pH value.

The release of hydrophobic drugs from CPT-loaded micelles was a rather complex process. Although it had attracted the interest of many researchers, there was still no comprehensive theory or model to fully explain this process up to date. At present, the most commonly applied release model is the semi-empirical Eq. (5) proposed by Ritger and Peppas [39,40].
(5)
M t
M ∞
=
kt n
(6)
log
M t
M ∞
= n log t + log k

The logarithmic form of the formula (5) is the formula (6). In the formula, M
t and M ∞ represent the absolute cumulative release at time t and infinite, respectively. n means release exponent, reflecting the release mechanism of the drug. While k means a constant related to the geometric characteristics and structure, approximately representing the release rate of the drug. There are three common models of drug release: the Fickian diffusion model, the swelling model, and the erosion model. For spherical particles, n = 0.43 is the Fickian diffusion mechanism, while n = 0.85 is the non-Fickian mechanism. n < 0.43 results from a combination of diffusion and erosion control, whereas 0.43 < n < 0.85 corresponds to an anomalous transport mechanism.

The experimental data of in vitro drug release was linearly fitted to further explore its release model. In Fig. 5D, the n of the drug release series equaled the slope of the line, while the k of the release rate constant equaled the intercept. n and k of parameters for the different pH conditions were listed in Table S1. From the fitting results, the process of drug release was divided into two phases of 0–12 h and 12–108 h, and both with consistent fitted data. Both phases of release series n < 0.43 at pH = 7.4, T = 37 °C & 30 °C, indicating the drug release was mainly controlled by diffusion-erosion. As pH < 5.0, n = 0.520 of the first stage mean the anomalous transport controlled release, while n = 0.287 of the second stage indicated that the drug release was controlled by diffusion-erosion. Under varying pH conditions, the release rate constant k gradually increased with a decrease in pH, indicating that the CPT release accelerated due to the protonation of the PDMAEMA block. Both phases of the drug release rate were lowest at pH = 7.4, because CPT was not only enable to stabilize itself in the core of the micelle with the protection of the brush-shaped hydrophilic shell's PEGMA, but also released only by slower diffusion. At pH = 5.0, k reached its maximum. This occurred because the increase in the degree of micellar protonation significantly accelerated the rate of CPT release.

The anti-tumor activity of blank micelles, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT against MCF-7 cell and HepG2 cells were measured by CCK8. The cytotoxicity results of blank micelles and AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag against MCF-7 cells (Fig. 6A) and HepG2 cells (Fig. 6C) showed that cell survival was approximately 70 % when the micelles concentration increased to 800 μg/mL, indicating polymer with lower toxicity and was safe for normal tissues. The viability of MCE-7 cells (Fig. 6B) and HepG2 cells (Fig. 6D) co-cultured with AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT decreased significantly with the increase concentration of CPT. More than 65 % of the cells were already killed at high concentrations (150 μg/mL) of CPT, indicating that CPT-loaded micelles could effectively kill cancer cells. Fig. 6B & D used statistics to show the significance of the cytotoxic changes. Compared with the control, the inhibitory effect of 150 μg/mL of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles on cancer cells was rather significant (***P < 0.001).

In the fluorescence microscopy experiments, it was observed that the HepG2 cells in the control group (Fig. 6E) maintained the integrity of their morphology, showing bright green fluorescence without any red fluorescence. However, after co-culturing with 150 μg/mL of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles for 48 h (Fig. 6F), we noticed that red fluorescence was enhanced, while green fluorescence was significantly diminished and cell fragmentation became severe, which was consistent with the outcomings from the cytotoxicity tests.

Overall, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles demonstrated promising potential for cancer therapy. However, the current inhibition rate of tumor cells is not significant. Future studies could explore optimization strategies such as regulating the ratio of hydrophilic/hydrophobic blocks and drug-loaded micelles formulations/methods to enhance an enhanced ability to kill tumor cells. Then, we could further conduct animal experiments to study the inhibitory effect and therapeutic efficacy of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT micelles on tumor tissues.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the bacterial growth curve was highly correlated with the concentration of AgNPs in LB media, the antibacterial activity was more pronounced with an increased concentration of AgNPs. Within the range of 0–800 μg/mL, bacterial growth was gradually inhibited as the concentration of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag increased. Compared to the blank micelles with only about 20 % antibacterial rate, the AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag at concentrations of 600 μg/mL and 800 μg/mL completely inhibited the growth of S. aureus and E. coli respectively, achieving inhibition rates as high as 93 %. The antibacterial rate of blank micelles (around 20 %) was attributed to alkali lignin, a natural polyphenol polymer that exhibited antibacterial properties by directly contacting bacteria, thus resulting in cell membrane rupture. Additionally, within a concentration range of 0–800 μg/mL, AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT exhibited antibacterial rates against S. aureus and E. coli that were approximately 15 % and 20 % higher than those of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag, respectively. Meanwhile, the growth of S. aureus and E. coli was inhibited by up to 90 % at AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT concentrations of 600 μg/mL and 800 μg/mL. The results indicated that AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT exhibited significantly effective antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli at concentrations of 600 μg/mL and 800 μg/mL, respectively. It also had the potential to provide a promising antibacterial effect in addressing the issue of cancer treatment.

In summary, we reported the first application of GQDs as excellent photocatalysts for blue light redox ATRP in DMF media. Notably, The polymerization process was controllable with lower copper (<500 ppm). AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 had a molecular weight of 46,884 and PDI as low as 1.07. AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2 micelles with the size of 40–110 nm were formed in an aqueous solution through self-assembly. It could prepare AgNPs in situ and encapsulate CPT to obtain AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT containing a good ability to stabilize AgNPs, high drug encapsulation efficiency, high efficient drug release, good cytotoxicity, and antibacterial capability, achieving the anti-tumor and anti-bacteria synergistic therapy in cancer sites. Moreover, it also provided solutions to solve the chemotherapeutic drug resistance of tumor cells caused by bacteria, which helped tackle two problems simultaneously. Furthermore, the more content of CPT or AgNPs, the better the anti-tumor and anti-bacterial effect of AL-(PNIPAM-PDMAEMA-PPEGMA)2@Ag@CPT in the micelles. Therefore, a series of structurally controllable multifunctional copolymers could be designed and synthesized by GQDs-catalyzed photo redox ATRP in the future, exerting great potentiality in cancer cure fields such as anti-tumor anti-bacteria synergistic therapy and integration of diagnosis and treatment.
